query: proportion of inorganic component in bone

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bones -- Structure and Function -- Bone Composition
retrieved_docs.content: The bone's extracellular matrix comprises collagen type I and crystals of the calcium phosphate mineral, calcium hydroxyapatite. The osteon contains the osteocytes, which are crucial to bone integrity and the remodeling process. Cement lines, also known as cement sheaths, delineate the osteons and have a higher mineral density than other parts of the bone matrix. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Extracellular Matrix
retrieved_docs.content: The bone extracellular matrix makes up 90% of the overall bone volume and consists of inorganic (mineral) and organic matrices. Inorganic bone matrix accounts for 99% of the body's storage of calcium, 85% of the phosphorus, and 40% to 60% of the magnesium and sodium. It is mainly in the form of hydroxyapatite [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] to provide the bone its strength, stiffness, and resistance to compressive forces. Organic bone matrix is secreted by osteoblasts and is predominantly type I collagen; this also contains glycoproteins, growth factors, and proteoglycans. Growth factors (such as osteocalcin, osteonectin, and bone sialoprotein) play important roles in osteoid formation, mineralization, and bone remodeling. The organic matrix gives bone its form and provides resistance to tensile forces. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphate is an abundant mineral found in the body. The body store of phosphate is 500 to 800 g, with 85% of the total body phosphate present in crystals of hydroxyapatite in the bone — about 10%  found in muscles and bones in association with proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids. The rest gets distributed in various compounds in the extracellular fluid (ECF) and intracellular fluid (ICF). Phosphate is predominantly an intracellular anion.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite is an inorganic mineral with a typical apatite lattice structure represented as (A10(BO4)6C2), where A, B, and C correspond to Ca, PO 4 , and OH, respectively. Pure hydroxyapatite contains 39.68% calcium and 18% phosphorus by weight, resulting in a Ca/P molar ratio of 1.67. Commercial hydroxyapatite products have Ca/P ratios that can be higher or lower than 1.67. This variation in the Ca/P ratio indicates a phase shift between tricalcium phosphate (TCP) and calcium oxide (CaO). Hydroxyapatite with a Ca/P ratio greater than 1.67 contains more CaO than TCP, while those with a lower ratio contain more TCP. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Once the osteoid is mineralized, the precursor cells get surrounded by organic intracellular substances called lacunae to become fully developed and matured into osteocytes. The mineral content is chiefly hydroxyapatite crystals composed of mainly calcium and phosphate ions but can occasionally have sodium and carbonite as well. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Enchondroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is a unique connective tissue type, being one that undergoes mineralization. Bone's inorganic component consists of calcium hydroxyapatite, which imparts strength and toughness. This mineral is also the body's main calcium (99%) and phosphate (85%) reservoir and stores 65% of its sodium and magnesium. Meanwhile, bone's organic component is comprised of bone cells and matrix proteins. The bone cells include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: The bone matrix can store several minerals, chiefly calcium and phosphorus as well as iron in the form of ferritin. Chondroitin sulfate, a carbohydrate moiety, is also a commonly found element in the matrices. Specific growth factors, including insulin-like growth factor or IGF-1, are housed in bone and then released periodically. pH balance is also regulated as bones may alter the composition of alkaline salts in the serum to maintain the optimal pH level. Moreover, osteocytes can engulf toxic molecules and heavy metals from the serum as a means of detoxification. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoblasts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is approximately 10% water, 30% organic, and 60% inorganic. The organic component is approximately 85 to 90% collagen (primarily type 1 -resisting tensile forces), proteoglycans (resisting compressive forces), non-collagenous proteins (osteocalcin and osteonectin) and glycoproteins (osteopontin). The inorganic component, or mineralized matrix, is composed of hydroxyapatite crystals [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2] that provides protection and support while serving as the body's repository for calcium and phosphate. [2] [3] [4] [5] Osteoblasts also indirectly regulate osteoclast formation and bone remodeling by cell-cell contact, paracrine signaling, and cell-bone matrix interaction. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite crystals are naturally present in the human bones and teeth. Hydroxyapatite crystals, as a bioactive ceramic, account for 65% to 70% of its weight in human bone. Furthermore, the architecture of the bone comprises type-I collagen as the organic component and hydroxyapatite as an inorganic component. Together, these 2 components create a nanoscale composite structure, where nano-hydroxyapatite is interspersed within the collagen network. This composite forms mineralized collagen and serves as the precursor for biological mineralized tissues, ranging from tendons and skin to hard tissues such as bone and teeth. Moreover, in bones, hydroxyapatite crystals are plate- or needle-shaped, measuring approximately 40 to 60 nm in length, 20 nm in width, and 1.5 to 5 nm in thickness. [3] The varying sizes and shapes of hydroxyapatite crystals contribute to the structural stability, hardness, and function of these tissues. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Introduction -- Phosphorus
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphorus is an extracellular fluid cation. Eighty-five percent of the total body phosphorus is in the bones and teeth in the form of hydroxyapatite; the soft tissues contain the remaining 15%. Phosphate plays a crucial role in metabolic pathways. It is a component of many metabolic intermediates and, most importantly, of ATP and nucleotides. Vitamin D3, PTH, and calcitonin regulate phosphate simultaneously with calcium. The kidneys are the primary avenue of phosphorus excretion. Phosphate imbalance is most commonly due to one of three processes: impaired dietary intake, gastrointestinal disorders, and deranged renal excretion. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is the fifth most common element in the body and the most prevalent cation. An average human body (70 kg) contains about 1 kg, or approximately 25 mol, of calcium. [5] The skeleton contains approximately 99% of the body’s calcium, predominantly as extracellular crystals of an unknown structure and a composition approaching hydroxyapatite. [4] Soft tissues and extracellular fluid contain about 1% of the body’s calcium. In blood, virtually all calcium is present in the plasma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Of the total phosphate in the body, 85% is n the bones and teeth,1% in the extracellular fluid and the remaining 14% is distributed in other tissues where it is an important constituent of cell membranes, nucleic acids, high energy phosphate esters (ATP) and intracellular signaling proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Bone -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is the primary anatomical structure comprising of the human skeletal system. Functionally, it assumes a significant mechanical role by the skeleton, and represents a stock of mineral salts to mobilize for maintenance of calcium and phosphorus homeostasis. It protects several vital organs (skull, vertebrae and rib cage). Through the medullary spaces, it hosts, the bone provides structural and functional support for hematopoiesis

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is a specialized complex, living connective tissue that supports the body and protects vital organs of the body. [1] [2] Impregnation of the extracellular matrix with the inorganic salts like calcium phosphate and carbonate provides hardness to the bone. [3] Types of Bones: Histologically, bones categorize into two types (1) cortical or compact bone and (2) cancellous bone or spongy bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite from natural sources is commonly fabricated from materials such as fishbone, coral, bovine bone, eggshell, and seashells through a calcination process. Hydroxyapatite produced from these natural sources is often nonstoichiometric due to the presence of trace ions. [13] [17] These traces of ions, which include cations such as Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Sr 2+ , Zn 2+ , and Al 3+ , as well as anions such as F - , Cl - , SO 4 2- , and CO 3 2- , are beneficial for promoting rapid bone regeneration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypophosphatemia -- Introduction -- Phosphorus Homeostasis
retrieved_docs.content: In general, phosphate levels increase through upregulated absorption in the intestines and decrease through renal excretion. Excess is stored in the bones, which act as a buffer to maintain a relatively stable total body content. A typical, nutritious diet provides 1000 to 2000 mg of phosphate daily. [11] [12] Of this, 600 mg to 1200 mg is absorbed via the intestines. [13] Phosphate exists primarily in the crystallized extracellular matrix of bones, where it is relatively stable and inert. In the absence of pathology, the homeostasis of bone phosphate is neutral, with resorption and deposition of approximately 3 mg/kg per day. [14] Bone homeostasis of phosphate is regulated primarily by the parathyroid hormone, vitamin D, and sex hormones. The serum phosphate levels are predominantly maintained by absorption via the kidneys, of which 70% to 80% occurs in the proximal convoluted tubule. [4] [15] Sodium-dependent phosphate type 2 (NaPi II) cotransporters in the proximal tubule are a primary site of regulation. The three types of type II transporters are type IIa, type IIb, and type IIc. Type IIa and IIc transporters are expressed predominately in the kidney. Type IIb transporters are expressed in the small intestine and control the dietary uptake of phosphate. [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate are both classified as calcium compounds, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of calcium‑containing substances. As members of this class, they share the fundamental chemical characteristic of providing bioavailable calcium and interacting with phosphate metabolism. Recognizing calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate as calcium compounds helps clarify their roles in medical nutrition, calcium supplementation, and the management of bone health and phosphate balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that include several distinct calcium salts. Among these are calcium glycerophosphate, which is a calcium‑based glycerophosphate compound, calcium acetate, a calcium salt of acetic acid, and tricalcium phosphate, a calcium phosphate used in various medical and nutritional contexts. All three entities share calcium as a central component, linking them together as members of the broader category of calcium compounds that are relevant for applications such as phosphate binding, calcium supplementation, and bone health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: Bone meal, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide are all classified as calcium compounds. As members of the calcium compounds category, each of these substances contains calcium and can be employed in medical practice for calcium‑related therapeutic purposes. Calcium acetate is commonly used as a phosphate binder in patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide serves as a source of elemental calcium that can be administered as a supplement or incorporated into topical preparations to support bone health and calcium balance. Bone meal provides both calcium and phosphorus, offering a natural source of these minerals that can aid in bone remodeling and nutritional supplementation. Recognizing that bone meal, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide belong to the broader group of calcium compounds assists clinicians in selecting appropriate calcium‑based interventions for various patient care needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 746
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Oyster Shell is linked to vitamin D through a specific, narrower relationship that suggests it may serve as a complementary source or enhancer of vitamin D activity. IRON/MULTIVITAMINS LIQUID lists vitamin D as an ingredient, and multivitamin tablets also contain vitamin D as a key component. The concept of inhibitory kostní resorpce is identified as a form of vitamin D activity that helps suppress bone resorption. Vitamin D is further noted to be related to هشاشة العظام (osteoporosis), underscoring its important role in maintaining bone health and preventing excessive bone loss. Together, these connections highlight vitamin D as a central factor in bone metabolism, with various supplements such as calcium oyster shell products, iron/multivitamin liquids, and multivitamin tablets providing it to support bone strength and reduce the risk of osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 727
retrieved_docs.content: IRON/MULTIVAMINS LIQUID and Multivitamin tablet both contain vitamin D as an ingredient, indicating that vitamin D is a common component of these supplement formulations. A specific form of vitamin D referred to as inhibitory kostní resorpce functions to inhibit bone resorption, highlighting its role in preserving bone integrity. Vitamin D is also related to هشاشة العظام (osteoporosis), underscoring the importance of adequate vitamin D intake for the prevention and management of bone‑weakening conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
retrieved_docs.content: together with iron supports erythropoiesis and hemoglobin synthesis. Consequently, clinicians prescribe these combined supplements for iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, or patients at risk of such hematologic disorders. **Vitamin K**, a fat‑soluble vitamin hierarchically related to the retinoid family, is incorporated into multivitamin‑iron tablets and into vitamin‑IV solution additives for parenteral nutrition, extending micronutrient coverage of both oral and intravenous regimens. **Vitamin D** occupies a central role in calcium and bone metabolism. It is the parent compound for the steroid‑derived hormone **calcitriol** and for analogues such as dihydrotachysterol and vitamin D₂, all grouped under “vitamin D and analogues.” Calcium oyster shell is linked as a natural source of vitamin D‑related calcium. Vitamin D and its analogues appear in oral multivitamin‑iron liquids, tablets, and IV nutrient mixes, where they inhibit bone resorption and address osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 565
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Oyster Shell is specifically linked to vitamin D, indicating a narrower, synergistic relationship between the calcium source and the vitamin that supports calcium absorption. IRON/MULTIVITAMINS LIQUID includes vitamin D as an ingredient, and multivitamin tablets also contain vitamin D, while vitamin D may be added as an additive in vitamin IV solutions. Vitamin D itself functions as an inhibitor of bone resorption (inhibitory kostní resorpce), a key mechanism that helps maintain bone density. Because of this role, vitamin D is directly related to osteoporosis (هشاشة العظام), highlighting its importance in the prevention and management of bone‑weakening conditions. Together, these formulations and the physiological actions of vitamin D underscore its central place in supporting skeletal health and addressing disorders such as osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2891
retrieved_docs.content: Mineral supplements represent a category of nutritional products that provide essential minerals for health maintenance, and they include both calcium protein preparations and bor (boron). Calcium protein preparations are formulations that combine calcium with protein to support bone strength and muscle function, while bor is a trace mineral that contributes to bone metabolism and hormone regulation. Both calcium protein preparations and bor are considered types of mineral supplements used to address mineral deficiencies and promote musculoskeletal health in patient care.

Understanding each component’s active versus inactive status, biochemical function, therapeutic role (e.g., bone health, metabolic support) and safety considerations (e.g., kidney‑stone risk) enables clinicians to evaluate supplement composition, ensure appropriate dosing and provide accurate patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: biotin, calcium pantothenate), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol) often paired with calcium sources (e.g., calcium oyster shell) to support calcium metabolism and inhibit bone resorption, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol), and essential minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine, selenium, chromium, molybdenum, vanadium, potassium iodide). Many formulations also contain omega‑3 fatty acids and antioxidants such as lutein and α‑lipoic acid. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin and calcium pantothenate function both independently and synergistically across diverse pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products. Their combined actions provide antioxidant protection, metabolic and lipid regulation, enzymatic support, immune competence, red‑blood‑cell formation, bone mineralization and overall nutritional adequacy, underscoring their broad relevance to patient care, therapeutic formulation design and clinical outcomes.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate calcium carbonate’s multifaceted presence in veterinary and human medicines—as an inert filler, a therapeutic calcium source, a contributor to electrolyte homeostasis, and a material foundation for biologically derived substances.

In multivitamin formulations vitamin D works synergistically with calcium, iron, vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E, the full B‑vitamin complex, and trace minerals to provide complementary nutritional benefits: calcium pantothenate supports cellular metabolism within the B‑vitamin spectrum, while vitamin D promotes skeletal health. Collectively, these nutrients—vitamin A (various chemical forms), niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) and vitamin D—are extensively utilized as active or inactive ingredients across oral multivitamin‑mineral products, prenatal supplements, DHA‑enriched blends, iron‑fortified formulations and topical dermatologic preparations. Their mechanisms of action (cellular modulation, enzyme interaction, antioxidant activity, phosphate chelation, inhibition of bone resorption) and their synergistic inclusion with iron, calcium, other vitamins and trace minerals underpin their therapeutic, preventive and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormone is the broader class that includes kalcitriol, a biologically active metabolite that is derived from vitamin D, making vitamin D the parent compound of kalcitriol. Calcium Oyster Shell is closely linked to vitamin D, reflecting a narrower but relevant relationship often seen in calcium‑vitamin D supplement formulations. Vitamin D is also incorporated as an ingredient in both IRON/MULTIVITAMINS LIQUID and multivitamin tablets, indicating its widespread use in combined micronutrient products. Functionally, vitamin D exerts an inhibitory effect on bone resorption (inhibitory kostní resorpce) and is directly related to osteoporosis (هشاشة العظام), underscoring its critical role in maintaining bone health and preventing bone loss. Together, these connections highlight vitamin D’s central position as a precursor to the steroid hormone kalcitriol, a component of various supplements, and a key regulator of skeletal metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.
